summary: Stancil reflects on the intoxicating but ultimately hollow experience of 'vibe coding' with AI tools, questioning whether the frenetic building of apps and prototypes constitutes real work or is merely a new form of vanity and addiction. He draws parallels to previous tech manias, suggesting the thrill of AI-assisted creation will inevitably fade, leaving people to reckon with what they actually built.
key_points: AI coding tools create an addictive cycle of rapid prototyping that loses coherence over time
The dream of being a solo founder with an army of AI agents may be someone else's dream repackaged
The line between productive building and compulsive hobby has blurred beyond recognition
Like remote work's mountain-house migration, the AI building mania will pass and people will have to assess where it took them
money_quotes: The current app is a blurry JPEG of its original ambition.
There were 14 versions; now there are 15 versions.
It is Jane Street, vibe coded. It is a billion dollars, and zero employees. Am I working? Yes, on someone else's dream.
He took to his computer, convinced himself of the profundity of his ideas and the urgency of their existence, and forgot to ask if the world really needed to hear so much from him.
This epidemic, too, will pass. No magic remains mesmerizing forever. The novelty will wear off. The subsidies will disappear.
key_insight: The AI coding revolution has created a seductive illusion of productivity and entrepreneurship that may ultimately prove to be a collective vanity project rather than meaningful work.
